findings.title: No authentication mechanism configured — security chain leaves all authenticated paths unauthenticated in practice
findings.description: SecurityConfig disables CSRF and requires authentication for all paths except /health via anyExchange().authenticated(). However, no authentication provider, UserDetailsService, or HTTP Basic/Bearer token configuration is present. Spring Security defaults to auto-generated HTTP Basic with a random password logged at startup. For a REST API serving Telegram bot webhooks, this means production endpoints are effectively protected only by a randomly regenerated credential that changes on every restart. This is intentional for Epic 01 (skeleton only), but the configuration does not communicate this intent, and there is no documented plan for when real auth will be wired in.
findings.impact: In a test/staging environment, a developer may start the service without noticing the random-password log line, leaving API endpoints accessible to anyone who brute-forces or discovers the default. In production (later epics), if auth wiring is forgotten, real endpoints will rely on this insecure fallback.
findings.recommendation: Add a comment in SecurityConfig explicitly documenting that authentication wiring is deferred and referencing the epic/task where it will be added. Optionally disable HTTP Basic explicitly (.httpBasic(basic -> basic.disable())) to prevent accidental fallback to the auto-generated credential, and instead return 401 with no WWW-Authenticate challenge until real auth is configured.

findings.title: No security event logging configured
findings.description: The SecurityConfig and application.properties define no logging configuration for security events (authentication failures, access denied events). Spring Security emits these at DEBUG level by default, making them invisible in standard INFO-level logs. No audit trail exists for unauthorized access attempts against anyExchange().authenticated() paths.
findings.impact: Security-relevant events (failed auth, 401 responses) are silently swallowed in default log output, making it impossible to detect scanning or probing activity in dev/staging environments.
findings.recommendation:
Add to application.properties:
logging.level.org.springframework.security=INFO
This surfaces security filter decisions at INFO level without verbosity of DEBUG. In later epics, add structured audit logging for authentication events.

findings.title: .gitignore missing backend/.idea/ and *.class pattern could match frontend artifacts
findings.description: The root .gitignore covers backend/build/ and backend/.gradle/ but does not explicitly exclude backend/.idea/ (IntelliJ project files at the backend module level). The .idea/ entry at line 74 covers only the root directory. Additionally, the *.class pattern at line 46 is global and could inadvertently suppress legitimate class-like files in non-Java directories, but more importantly backend/.idea/ containing local run configurations may expose environment variable values or local path information if accidentally committed.
findings.impact: IntelliJ run configurations inside backend/.idea/ may contain environment variable overrides, JVM arguments, or local connection strings that developers add during development. These could be committed and leak internal topology details.
findings.recommendation: The existing .idea/ entry at line 74 only matches a directory named .idea at the repository root. Add a pattern that covers nested .idea directories: **/.idea/ or specifically backend/.idea/
